benchmark: Adaptive retrieval — does a system's memory improve held-out retrieval?
status: NEGATIVE RESULT. Neither synaptic's Hebbian reinforcement nor any memory design we tried produces a query-conditional improvement. An earlier revision of this repo claimed OmniFuse won this axis; that claim was WRONG and has been reverted.
why_this_axis: It is the deepest difference between the projects: synaptic-*memory* is stateful and learns (Hebbian reinforcement of nodes/edges feeding resonance-ranked search); OmniFuse is a stateless one-shot retriever. Neither side had measured whether the dynamics improve retrieval QUALITY — synaptic's own memory eval (eval/scripts/memory_operating_poc.py) is a contract smoke-gate.

protocol.split: queries split deterministically 50/50 into FEEDBACK (F) and HELD-OUT EVAL (E); E is never searched during feedback
protocol.feedback_signal: identical for both systems — replay F, report which of the retrieved top-10 were relevant
protocol.metric: MRR@10 on E, before (cold) and after (warm)
protocol.leakage_check: zero exact-duplicate and zero near-duplicate (Jaccard>=0.8) queries between F and E
protocol.mandatory_controls: shuffled / random-q placebos (same documents, same volume of remembered text, broken pairing) and a covered/uncovered split of E. Without these the benchmark reports a win that is not there.

synaptic_verdict: Neutral to harmful in every measurement. Reinforcing nodes and edges learns a QUERY-INDEPENDENT prior — 'this document tends to be relevant' — but relevance is a property of a (query, document) pair.

beta_posterior_odds.detail: score *= (hits+1)/(misses+1). Judged documents sink relative to never-shown ones: most retrieved documents are irrelevant.

empirical_bayes_lift.detail: shrink the posterior to the corpus base rate so an average document is exactly neutral. The most 'correct' design, and the worst — calibration was never the problem.

query_conditional_text_memory.idea: a confirmed query becomes part of what the document is *about*: stored as text, indexed as its own BM25F field
query_conditional_text_memory.verdict: VOID — the apparent gain survives every placebo
query_conditional_text_memory.decisive_evidence: On MIRACL-ko, held-out queries whose relevant documents remembered NOTHING still gained +0.0441. A query-conditional memory cannot move those. Attaching a RANDOM feedback query to each confirmed document scored as well as attaching the real one (+0.0665 vs +0.0618).
query_conditional_text_memory.the_actual_mechanism: Injecting query text into documents raises the document frequency of query vocabulary, which deflates its IDF corpus-wide. MIRACL-ko is exactly the corpus that prefers weaker IDF emphasis (0.9052 at idf_pow=1.5 -> 0.9489 at 1.0), so the 'memory' was an accidental, uncontrolled `idf_pow` reduction.
query_conditional_text_memory.control_that_isolates_it: Memory made of tokens that can never match any query (shared or per-document unique) yields delta = +0.0000 exactly. The effect requires the memory text to match queries — i.e. it flows through term statistics, not through the (query, document) pairing.

conclusion: No memory mechanism measured here — synaptic's or ours — improves held-out retrieval in a query-conditional way. The OmniFuse `Feedback` feature was reverted. The benchmark is kept, with placebo and covered/uncovered controls mandatory, because the naive version of it produces a convincing false positive.
honest_limitations: synaptic was driven through graph.search + graph.reinforce (FTS, no embedder). Its EvidenceSearch path, embedder, reranker and consolidation cascade were not measured; a different configuration might behave differently.

Two corpora and two splits. A larger sweep might find a regime where query-conditional memory transfers — NFCorpus (82% of relevant documents serve more than one query) was chosen precisely because transfer should be easiest there, and it did not appear.
Absolute cold MRR here is computed on half the queries and does not match the static suite's full-set numbers.
